question_title: What will be the result of the 2026 Florida Republican gubernatorial primary?
summary: Byron Donalds is the overwhelming frontrunner in the 2026 FL GOP gubernatorial primary: Trump endorsement, $90M raised, ~96% on Polymarket to win the nomination, and polls showing him at 46-54% with opponents (Collins, Fishback, Renner) in single digits and ~38% undecided. "Donalds loses" is very unlikely (~4%). The real uncertainty is the winning margin. Undecideds tend to break toward an endorsed, well-funded frontrunner, but three opponents remain on the ballot splitting the opposition. I modeled final vote share as normal(0.69, 0.095), producing a spread across the top three buckets centered on 65-75%, with meaningful mass on ≥75% and 55-65%.

factors.supports: Donalds wins, with >55% and ≤65% of the vote

reasoning:
Forecast submitted. 

**2026 Florida Republican gubernatorial primary — result distribution:**

| Outcome | Probability |
|---|---|
| Donalds wins, ≥75% | 25% |
| Donalds wins, >65% and <75% | 38% |
| Donalds wins, >55% and ≤65% | 26% |
| Donalds wins, ≤55% | 7% |
| Donalds loses | 4% |

**Reasoning:** Byron Donalds is the dominant frontrunner — Trump endorsement, $90M raised, ~96% on Polymarket to win the nomination, and polls showing 46-54% versus single-digit rivals (Collins, Fishback, Renner). The winner question is essentially settled (~96% he wins), so the forecast is really about margin. With ~38% undecided likely breaking toward the endorsed, best-funded candidate — but three opponents still splitting the opposition on the ballot — I modeled final vote share as centered near 69% with wide uncertainty, placing the mode in the 65-75% bucket while leaving meaningful mass on both the ≥75% and 55-65% tails.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves as the result of the [2026 Republican primary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Florida_gubernatorial_election#Republican_primary) in the Florida gubernatorial election, according to official results or [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions).

input.anchor: Trump-endorsed dominant primary frontrunners typically win 60-80%; Polymarket ~96% to win nomination.
input.assessment: Donalds is the overwhelming frontrunner. The question is purely about margin. Polls at 46-54% with heavy undecided; final share likely 60-78% as undecideds break to the endorsed, well-funded frontrunner. Three weak opponents remain, splitting the opposition. Modeled final share as normal(0.69, 0.095), yielding a spread across the top three buckets with small mass on lose (~4%).
input.tool_audit: research() returned rich, current polling and market data. Sandbox used for Monte Carlo. No failures.
input.process_reflection: Straightforward MC question; scaffolding worked well. Research sub-agent gave everything needed in one call.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
I read the full trace (research sub-agent, Monte Carlo modeling, and final bucketing) and verified the factors against tool results.

**Grounding check — all core claims supported, no hallucinations:**
- Donalds ~96% Polymarket to win nomination: grounded (stable 95.5–96.6% July 1–7).
- Polls (Emerson 46%/39% undecided; AIF 54%/+46; Change Research +39; Fabrizio 54%): all grounded in research data_points.
- $90M raised; three single-digit opponents (Collins, Fishback, Renner) on ballot; Casey DeSantis never entered: all grounded.

**No double-counting that flips direction, no wrong-direction factors.** Factor logit signs are directionally consistent with descriptions. Factors 1 and 2 both touch Donalds' dominance, but 1 is win-probability/endorsement and 2 is the vote-split dynamic — acceptable given both feed the same margin bucket.

**Resolution criteria:** Provided in the question (not MISSING) and point to the Wikipedia primary-results page. No recovery fetch required. Buckets are handled correctly; the Monte Carlo output maps cleanly to the tentative estimate (≥75%: .253, 65-75%: .384, 55-65%: .256, ≤55%: .067, loses: .04 → rounded to 25/38/26/7/4).

**No pre-publication-event problem:** This is a forward-looking "what will the result be" question; the primary (Aug 18) has not occurred. Factors treat polls as base-rate/forward evidence, not as already-resolving.

**Monte Carlo window:** No regime-spanning drift issue — the model is a hand-specified normal(0.69, 0.095) over vote share, not drift estimated from a contaminated time series.

**Independent probability estimate:** I concur closely. The hard part is the margin distribution, not the winner. Given a Trump-endorsed frontrunner at 46–54% in polls with ~38% undecided and three single-digit opponents, a final share centered ~69–72% with wide spread is well-supported by historical dominant-frontrunner primaries (typically 65–82%). My own distribution would be near-identical, perhaps shifting 2–4pp from the 55-65% bucket toward ≥75% (undecideds breaking to the endorsed, best-funded candidate tend to inflate the winner's share), but this is a magnitude quibble within noise, not a directional error. The ~4% "loses" mass is reasonable against ~96% Polymarket.

**Self-assessment quality:** The counterargument genuinely engages both tails with specific mechanisms (clear 75% vs. opponents holding him to 55-65%), not a token restatement. confidence_in_estimate = 0.60 is well-calibrated for a margin question with real bucket uncertainty. The final distribution stays consistent with the stated 60–80% anchor — no unjustified anchor departure.

**Minor note (non-blocking):** The agent's tool_audit says "no failures," and the session ground truth is 0/21 errors — consistent at the session level. However, the nested research sub-agent did hit several failed calls (asknews 403 credit/tier limits, WebFetch 403s on Wikipedia and Ballotpedia). These were recovered via alternate searches and did not degrade the evidence base, so the forecast is unaffected. Worth flagging only as a small transparency gap.

Overall: evidence chain is sound, math matches the model, distribution is evidence-driven and calibrated. Approve.
